coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of permanganate and hydrazine by a fluorescence off-on strategy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhi, Shumin
Yang, Xueyun
Yao, Cheng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized through one step hydrothermal reaction using ginkgo fruit and ethylenediamine as precursors. The luminescence of the N-CDs was speculated to be related with the electron transition of C=N bonds derived from grafting amines groups onto the the functional groups on the surface. MnO4 − could selectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs due to the formation of N-CDs-MnO4 − complex on the surface. The linear relation between quenching efficiency and MnO4 − concentration ranged from 1 to 60 µmol/L with a detection limit of 0.64 µmol/L. In the presence of hydrazine (N2H4), MnO4 − was reduced to Mn2+ and the fluorescence of carbon dots was recovered. Results indicated that it can act as a secondary fluorescent probe for quantitative analysis of N2H4 in the scope of 0.5 to 14 µmol/L with a detection limit of 0.384 µmol/L. The N-CDs were successfully applied to analyze MnO4 − and N2H4 in real samples with satisfactory recoveries (97.34-104.0% and 97.56-104.4%, respectively), implying good applicability in practical analysis.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of MnO4 − and N2H4 in solutions

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of NCDs synthesis and the fluorescence assays of MnO4 − and N2H4.
(a) TEM images of N-CDs; (b) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs
XPS spectrum of N-CDs (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s.

(a)UV-vis absorption,excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs, inset: photos of the N-CDs under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right) in water; (b)Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths

(a) Response of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs (0.31 mg/mL) after the addition of MnO4 − (60 µmol/L) and other different ions (60 µmol/L). (b) Response of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/MnO4 − (0.31 mg/mL) after the addition of N2H4 (12 µmol/L) and other different ions (60 µmol/L)

(a) Effects of differing MnO4 − concentrations on N-CDs (0.31 mg/mL) fluorescence intensity; (b) Relationship between quenching efficiency and MnO4 − concentration; (c) Effects of differing N2H4 concentrations on N-CDs/MnO4 − fluorescence intensity; (d) Relationship between recovery and N2H4 concentration; (e) the photograph presented the N-CDs solutions and N-CDs solutions after adding different concentrations of MnO4 - under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (f) the photograph presented the N-CDs/MnO4 - solutions and N-CDs/MnO4 - solutions after adding different concentrations of N2H4 under daylight and 365 nm UV light.

(a) excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs, UV-vis absorption spectra of MnO4 −; (b) 2.48 mg/mL N-CDs fluorescence lifetime in the absence (black line) and presence of 1 µmol/L MnO4 − (red line). (c) Stern-Volmer curves for the N-CDs/MnO4 − system at three different temperatures; (d) UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CDs,MnO4 −,N-CDs/MnO4 − and the sum absorption spectrum between N-CDs and MnO4 −
Analytical results for determination of MnO4 − in Yangtze River water sample
Analytical results for determination of N2H4 in Yangtze River water sample

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon dots for sensitive detection of permanganate and hydrazine by a fluorescence off-on strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized through one step hydrothermal reaction using ginkgo fruit and ethylenediamine as precursors. The luminescence of the N-CDs was speculated to be related with the electron transition of C=N bonds derived from grafting amines groups onto the the functional groups on the surface. MnO4 − could selectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs due to the formation of N-CDs-MnO4 − complex on the surface. The linear relation between quenching efficiency and MnO4 − concentration ranged from 1 to 60 µmol/L with a detection limit of 0.64 µmol/L. In the presence of hydrazine (N2H4), MnO4 − was reduced to Mn2+ and the fluorescence of carbon dots was recovered. Results indicated that it can act as a secondary fluorescent probe for quantitative analysis of N2H4 in the scope of 0.5 to 14 µmol/L with a detection limit of 0.384 µmol/L. The N-CDs were successfully applied to analyze MnO4 − and N2H4 in real samples with satisfactory recoveries (97.34-104.0% and 97.56-104.4%, respectively), implying good applicability in practical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Permanganate (MnO4 −) has strong oxidizing properties that can trigger bacteria and other organisms to release renascent oxygen immediately [1]. It is a traditional disinfectant with low toxicity, few or mild side effects, and requires only small doses [2]. MnO4 − is toxic and, when inhaled, can cause respiratory damage [1]. Eye contact irritates the conjunctiva and causes burning pain in severe cases. Concentrated solutions, or crystal, corrode the skin [3]. Hydrazine (N2H4) is an important industrial chemical with strong polarity, corrosivity and reducibility. It has been widely used in various fields, such as pharmaceutical, agriculture and chemical industry [4–5]. However, N2H4 is toxic and carcinogenic. It can be easily inhaled via mouth and skin because of good water solubility and volatility. The gradually accelerating mutagenic effects can lead to irreversible damage of organs, including kidneys, liver, lungs, central nerves and respiratory system [6–8]. In the manufacture, transportation, application and disposal of N2H4, a micro leakage can probably cause environmental pollution and threaten human health [9]. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop an effective and reliable analytical method for tracing MnO4 − and N2H4 in daily life.

Until now, various analytical techniques for respectively detecting MnO4 − and N2H4 have been reported, mainly including liquid chromatography [10], electroanalysis [11], and optical [12]methods [3]. Among these methods, optical techniques like fluorescence and UV-vis absorption methods have attracted considerable attention due to their rapid response, high sensitivity, practicality, simplicity and low cost [13]. Recently, Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention because of their enormous promising applications in the fields of sensing, fluorescent ink, catalysis, bio-imaging, drug delivery, solar, and optoelectronic devices owing to their [12]unique optical properties. The excellent properties of CDs in terms of photostability, chemically inert, cost effective, nontoxic, eco-friendly, biocompatible, colloidal stability, and the tunable photoluminescence makes it the next generation's green nanomaterials to replace conventionally used fluoroprobes, such as organic dyes, conjugated polymer, quantum dots, upconverting nanoparticles and more suitable for many novel applications than that of conventional fluoroprobes [14]. Thus, it is particularly necessary to construct a good selective, highly sensitive, and easily-operated multifunctional nanoplatform for detecting MnO4 − and N2H4.

On the other hand, green synthesis of fluorescent CDs using natural precursors are getting more attention [15]. Ginkgo fruit contains flavonoids, phenolic acids, terpene lactones, reducing sugars, vitamins and various trace elements [16]. Interestingly, many studies have reported that natural flavonoids and their glycosides with some specific structural motifs can bind to Pb2+ with high selectivity [17–19]. Flavonoid moiety incorporated CDs have nitrogen-containing groups, hydroxyl groups, and carboxyl groups, which also can specifically recognize Fe3+ [20]. Based on the extensive study on fluorescence of CDs, it is widely believed that the presence of nitrogen increases the Fermi level by enriching electron density and reducing surface defects, and not only improves the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), but also greatly adjusts its optical, surface and local chemical properties [21]. The QY of N-CDs is 24.6%, which is 8 times higher than that of CDs without nitrogen in this research. Therefore suitable controlling the functional groups, compositions and structures of CDs are indispensable for expanding their sensing applications, as well as excavating their complicated luminescence mechanisms [22]. Ma et al. synthesized nitrogen, sulfur, boron and flavonoid moiety co-incorporated carbon dots for sensitive fluorescence detection of pesticides [21]. In this work, N-CDs has a large number of functional groups containing carbon, nitrogen and oxygen on the surface after inheriting from the precursor. In particular, flavonoids contain a large number of acyl groups. It is speculated that the electron transition of C=N bond is generated after the graft of amine group to N-CDs surface functional group, which is related to the luminescence of N-CDs. MnO4 − could selectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs due to the formation of N-CDs-MnO4 − complex on the surface. Furthermore, N2H4 can reduce MnO4 − to Mn2+. Compared with other reported methods, this work is low cost, good sensitivity, fast response, and visualization property. This approach was used for efficient determination of MnO4 − and N2H4 in real water samples. The schematic diagram of N-CDs synthesis and the detections of MnO4 − and N2H4 appeared in Scheme 1 .

NaCl, FeCl3·6H2O, MnCl2, FeCl2, CaCl2, KCl, AlCl3, MgCl2, NaS2O3, NaNO2, HClO, H2O2, H2CO3, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). KMnO4 was gained from the National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China). hydrazine was taken from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ cm) from a Millipore Auto-pure WR600A system (Millipore Ltd, USA) was used in all experiments. All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Fresh Ginkgo fruit were obtained from trees located in Nanjing Tech University.

UV-vis absorption spectra were measured using a Persee TU-1900 double beam UV-vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence analysis was carried out by an F97XP spectrofluorometer fluorescence spectrometer with 10 nm slit width for both excitation and emission spectra using xenon flash lamp. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out on a JEM-2100 Plus with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrophotometer with a wave number range of 4000-500 cm−1. The pH adjustments were performed by a PHS-3C digital pH meter. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo Fisher K-Alpha electron spectrometer for analyzing the elemental composition and structure of N-CDs. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were acquired by Edinburgh Instruments FLS980 spectrometer. LeiBoEr high speed centrifuge (Beijing Centrifuge Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used for separation crude products of N-CDs.

N-CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. First, the shell of ginkgo fruit picked from the tree was removed, the fresh ginkgo fruit was grinded in a mortar. Subsequently 5.0g of ground pulp and 3.2g ethylenediamine were simultaneously added into 20mL deionized water and mixed well. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave at 210 °C for 25 h for complete carbonization. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction product was centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 20 min to remove the precipitates and obtain N-CDs solution. A 0.22 µm microporous membrane was used to remove large particle impurities. The solution was then purified by ultrasonic dialysis using a 500 MWCO dialysis bag for 48 h to remove the unreacted precursors and the by-products from the synthetic procedure. After spin-drying, the purified N-CDs were conserved at 4°C for later use.

To optimize the conditions of synthesizing N-CDs, a series of experiments were designed and performed. As revealed in Fig. S1(a), (b) and (c), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs varied accompanied by the reaction time, amount of ethylenediamine and reaction temperature, demonstrating that preparing N-CDs was regulated by these selected conditions. Consequently, 25 h, 3.2 g ethylenediamine and 210℃ were identified as the optimal conditions towards preparing N-CDs.

Under the optimum parameters, the obtained N-CDs solution was firstly diluted with deionized water to obtain N-CDs suspension (0.31 mg/mL). In a typical detection of MnO4 -, 1980 µL of N-CDs suspension and 20 µL of MnO4 - with varying concentrations were mixed uniformly and then placed for 30 s at room temperature (25℃). For N2H4 detection, 20 µL N2H4 of different concentrations were added to 1980 µL N-CDs-MnO4 - complex (MnO4 - concentration 60 µmol/L) at room temperature for 1 min. The concentration of N2H4 in the final system was 0-70 µmol/L. Fluorescence spectra were measured under 398 nm excitation. The selectivity for probe was measured by adding other fifteen kinds of metal ions (including Fe3+,Mn2+,Ca2+,K+,Na+,Mg2+,Al3+,Fe2+,MnO4 −,S2O3 2−,NO2 −) .

The morphology and diameter distribution of N-CDs was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) [23]. As indicated in Fig. 1 a, these N-CDs had an almost globular shape with the average particle size of 2.35±0.3 nm and superior monodispersibility [23]. The size distribution is consistent with a Gaussian function (Fig. 1a(I)) [24]. The DLS result indicated that the hydrate particle size of N-CDs was approximately 1.22 nm (Fig. S2), much smaller than the size examined by HR-TEM, which revealed the excellent hydrophilicity of N-CDs. The surface functional groups were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of N-CDs. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b) of the N-CDs displayed the peaks at 3500-3000 cm−1 are attributed to O-H/N-H stretching vibrations. The C=O bonds can be observed at 1715 cm−1. The peaks at 1649, 1421, and 1143 cm−1 are attributed to C=C/C=N, C-N, and C-C bonds, suggesting the successful adulteration of nitrogen through amines groups grafting onto the CDs, and the C=O groups of N-CDs originated from flavonoid have been completely transferred to C=N. The peak at 1036 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching vibration C-O-C group [25], which further confirms the flavonoid and lactones incorporated chemical structures of N-CDs. It is clearly seen that absorption peaks of N-CDs around 1020 cm−1/1190 cm−1 and 2916 cm−1 result from the stretching vibrations of C-O and C-H. A peak at about 1328 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of C-H [25]. The presence of various hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl on the surface of N-CDs is beneficial to enhance their hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solutions.

In order to further study the element composition of N-CDs, XPS measurement and analysis was carried out. The full spectra showed that three typical peaks, namely C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (399 eV), and O 1s (532 eV) (Fig. 2 a). The C 1s band can be fitted into four peaks, which reveal the presence of C=C (284.2), C=N (284.3 eV), C−O/C−N (285.7 eV), and O=C−O (288.4 eV) [26] (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution N1s spectrum has three peaks at 398.6, 399.4 and 400.6 eV, which are attributed to N−H, pyridine N and graphite N, respectively [26](Fig. 2c). The high-resolution O1s spectrum has three peaks at 531.4, 532.2 and 533.1 eV, corresponding to C=O, C−O and O−C=O [27] (Fig. 2d) .

Additionally, the corresponding UV-visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were studied. The typical peak at 280 nm and 340 nm could be responsible for the n→π* transition of the C=O band and n→π* electron transition of C=N bonds [26, 28] (Fig. 3 a). Moreover, from the FL spectra of as-obtained N-CDs, it clearly exhibited that the optimal emission centered at 462 nm under an excitation of 398 nm. The emission peaks of the N-CDs are shifted when the excitation wavelength changed from 360 to 450 nm (Fig. 3b). The excitation-dependent PL behavior may be due to their surface defects, different particle sizes, or surface light-emitting sites [29].

To the best of our knowledge, most of fluorescent CDs can be affected by numerous factors (ionic strengths, continuous UV light irradiation, pH and storage time) in a complex biological environment [30]. The emission intensity of the N-CDs is nearly unchanged even under continuous ultraviolet light irradiation for 1.5 h or ultrahigh concentration of NaCl solution (1.0 mol/L), respectively (Fig. S3a,b). The emission intensity of the N-CDs remained stable when the pH ranging from 1 to 10, but decrease dramatically at higher (pH = 11) (Fig. S3c). The influence of the pH on the fluorescence emission of the N-CDs may be due to the protonation-deprotonation of the -OH and -NH2 functional groups [30]. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs remains almost unchanged after 6 months of storage, which proves that it has good stability (Fig. S3d).

In order to optimize the MnO4 − and N2H4 detection conditions, the reaction pH and times were investigated. When solution pH values changed between 1 and 13, MnO4 − quenching efficiency to N-CDs first increased and then decreased. When the pH value was 1.5, the quenching efficiency reached a maximum value (Fig. S4a). The pH effect is probably due to the interaction between N-CDs and MnO4 −. At higher pH values, the quenching dropped very significantly presumably due to charge repulsion. N2H4 quenching efficiency to N-CDs/MnO4 − first increased and then decreased, When solution pH values changed between 2 and 12. When the pH value was 6.5, the quenching efficiency reached a maximum value (Fig. S4b). Thus, the optimal pH value was set at 6.5 for subsequent studies. When the reaction time was 30 s, quenching efficiency reached a maximum, so the optimal detection time of MnO4 − was 30 s (Fig. S4c). When the reaction time was 1 min, the effect of N2H4 on N-CDs/MnO4 − fluorescence recovery reached a maximum (Fig. S4d).

Detection selectivity is a critical parameter in measuring and evaluating fluorescent sensor performance. Under otherwise identical conditions, the following were tested MnO4 − detection method selectivity: Fe3+,Mn2+,Ca2+,K+,Na+,Mg2+,Al3+,Fe2+,MnO4 −,S2O3 2−,NO2 −,ClO−,H2O2,CO3 2−,HPO4 2−,H2PO4 −. Other cations or anions caused nearly no interference to N-CDs fluorescence quenching (Fig. S5a). After adding MnO4 − solution, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is still obvious. In addition, when MnO4 − coexists with other ions, the interference of other ions on the quenching effect of the N-CDs/MnO4 − system can be ignored (Fig. 4 a). The results show that N-CDs have better selectivity for MnO4 −. The effects of above ions on N-CDs/MnO4 − fluorescence recovery were examined to determine N-CDs/MnO4 − selectivity to N2H4 determination (Fig. S5b). The presence of these interfering species appears to have no effects on N2H4 detection. In the presence of other ions, after adding equal N2H4, the fluorescence of the entire system resumes (Fig. 4b). Therefore, this method can realize the selective determination of MnO4 − and N2H4 in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of MnO4 − and N2H4 in solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence of the N-CDs could be quenched gradually by increasing the concentration of MnO4 - in the range of 0–240 µmol/L (Fig. 5a). As displayed in Fig. 5b, (F0 - F)/F0 demonstrates a good linear relationship with MnO4 - concentration, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs solutions in the absence and presence of MnO4 -. The fluorescence response can be described by the following Stern-Volmer’s equation: (F0 - F)/F0 = 0.00820CMnO4- + 0.0710 The coefficients (R2) estimated by linear fitting were found to be 0.999 in the ranges of 1-60 µmol/L. The detection limit (S/N = 3) is 0.640 µmol/L.

Due to the redox reaction between MnO4 − and N2H4, MnO4 − can be reduced to be Mn species with lower valent. Since Mn species with lower valent do not affect the fluorescence of N-CDs, the fluorescence of N-CDs can be recovered (Fig. 5c). When N2H4 concentration reached 80 µmol/L, the recovery nearly ceased. There was a good linear relationship between (F1 - F0)/F0 and the concentration of N2H4 ranged from 0.5 to 14 µmol/L (Fig. 5d). The detection limit (S/N = 3) was 0.384 µmol/L. The recovery equation is (F1 - F0)/F0 = 0.0184C - 0.00831, R2 = 0.997. F0 and F1 are N-CDs/MnO4 − fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of N2H4, respectively. As shown in Table S1 and S2, the detection limit of our proposed strategy is lower than that of other strategies, indicating the high sensitivity of N-CDs toward MnO4 − and N2H4 detection. The proposed method herein is simple and cost effective, and sufficient to monitor MnO4 − and N2H4 at the extreme low level.

Possible quenching mechanisms were further evaluated based on the optical properties of MnO4 − and the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the UV-vis absorption spectrum of MnO4 − and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CDs had some overlap, indicating MnO4 − absorbed N-CDs excitation and emission. The results show that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs is due to a MnO4 − inner filter effect mechanism. The Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-CDs in the absence and presence of MnO4 − showed a high degree of consistency in the Fig. 6b. The decay trace is fitted using biexponential functions as follows: Y(t) = A1exp (−t/τ1) + A2 exp(−t/τ2), where A1 and A2 are the corresponding amplitudes of time-resolved decay lifetime, respectively; τ1 and τ2 are the time constants of the two radiative decay channels. In the absence and presence of MnO4 −, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs is 4.8561 ns and 5.0326 ns, respectively, which further suggests the occurrence of IFE. Fluorescence quenching was also fitted to the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = KSV [Q] + 1. KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] is the MnO4 − concentration. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and presence of MnO4 −, respectively. It can be seen in the Fig. 6c that as the temperature increases, the KSV value gradually decreases. As shown in Fig. 6d, the spectra of N-CDs/MnO4 - dided not coincide with the line formed by adding the absorbance values of N-CDs and MnO4 - and the new absorption peaks appeared at 230 nm by introducing MnO4 -. Therefore, the formation of the non-fluorescence complex through the interaction between N-CDs and the quenchers lead to the absorption spectrum of N-CDs changed, which indicate a static quenching process. N-CDs’zeta potentials were positive (1.02 mV), making an easy binding with MnO4 - and it became -0.88 mV after adding MnO4 -, which confirmed the interaction between the N-CDs and MnO4 - (Fig. S6(b)). Furthermore, in the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs-MnO4 - and N-CDs (Fig. S6(a)). The 1631 cm-1 peak describes the stretching vibration of the C=N. After the introduction of MnO4 -, the peak is significantly weakened or even disappeared. It is speculated that this is because the C=N on N-CDs surface interact with MnO4 - to form complex, and the further introduction of N2H4 destroys the complex, thus restoring their N-CDs fluorescence.

In brief, the electron transition of C=N bonds derived from grafting amines groups onto the flavonoid-related moieties effectively enhance photoluminescence quantum yield and intrinsically improve sensing performance of N-CDs. Results indicated that because of the inner filter effect and static quenching effect, quantitative detection of MnO4 − levels could be readily achieved by as-developed N-CDs. MnO4 − is reduced into Mn2+ by N2H4 and results in fluorescence reinstatement. The CDs were successfully employed to monitor MnO4 − and N2H4 from tap water and lake water. In view of the limitations of N-CDs-based assay, the studies on probes with well-defined chemical structures will be emphasized in future work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.greeac.2022.100022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile Preparation of Boron and Nitrogen Codoped Green Emission Carbon Quantum Dots for Detection of Permanganate and Captopril
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Jiang, D. Qin, G. Mo, J. Feng, X. Zheng, B. Deng, Facile Preparation of Boron and Nitrogen Codoped Green Emission Carbon Quantum Dots for Detection of Permanganate and Captopril, Anal. Chem., 91 (2019) 11455-11460.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Formation of assimilable organic carbon during oxidation of natural waters with ozone, chlorine dioxide, chlorine, permanganate, and ferrate
Water Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Ramseier, A. Peter, J. Traber, U. von Gunten, Formation of assimilable organic carbon during oxidation of natural waters with ozone, chlorine dioxide, chlorine, permanganate, and ferrate, Water Res., 45 (2011) 2002-2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright Mn-doped carbon dots for the determination of permanganate and L-ascorbic acid by a fluorescence on-off-on strategy
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chu, G. Ning, Z. Zhou, Y. Liu, Q. Xiao, S. Huang, Bright Mn-doped carbon dots for the determination of permanganate and L-ascorbic acid by a fluorescence on-off-on strategy, Microchim. Acta, 187 (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for hydrazine based on novel cyclization mechanism and its application in living cells
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Shi, F. Huo, J. Chao, C. Yin, A ratiometric fluorescent probe for hydrazine based on novel cyclization mechanism and its application in living cells, Sens. Actuators B, 260 (2018) 609-616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for hydrazine detection with large fluorescence change ratio and its application for fluorescence imaging in living cells
Spectrochim. Acta Part A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Liu, K. Liu, M. Tian, W. Lin, A ratiometric fluorescent probe for hydrazine detection with large fluorescence change ratio and its application for fluorescence imaging in living cells, Spectrochim. Acta Part A, 212 (2019) 42-47.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Peroxidase Substrates Stimulate the Oxidation of Hydralazine to Metabolites Which Cause Single-Strand Breaks in DNA
Chem. R. Toxicol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.A. Reilly, S.D. Aust, Peroxidase Substrates Stimulate the Oxidation of Hydralazine to Metabolites Which Cause Single-Strand Breaks in DNA, Chem. R. Toxicol., 10 (1997) 328-334.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple two-output near-infrared fluorescent probe for hydrazine detection in living cells and mice
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Lu, X. Shi, Y. Ma, W. Fan, Y. Lu, Z. Wang, C. Fan, A simple two-output near-infrared fluorescent probe for hydrazine detection in living cells and mice, Sens. Actuators B, 258 (2018) 42-49.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of Hydrazine-Induced Aggregation on the Electrochemical Detection of Platinum Nanoparticles
Langmuir

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.E.F. Kleijn, B. Serrano-Bou, A.I. Yanson, M.T.M. Koper, Influence of Hydrazine-Induced Aggregation on the Electrochemical Detection of Platinum Nanoparticles, Langmuir, 29 (2013) 2054-2064.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A generic approach for the determination of trace hydrazine in drug substances using in situ derivatization-headspace GC–MS
J. Pharmaceut. Biomed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sun, L. Bai, D.Q. Liu, A generic approach for the determination of trace hydrazine in drug substances using in situ derivatization-headspace GC–MS, J. Pharmaceut. Biomed., 49 (2009) 529-533.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free carbon dots from water hyacinth leaves as a highly fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of borax
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Prathumsuwan, P. Jaiyong, I. In, P. Paoprasert, Label-free carbon dots from water hyacinth leaves as a highly fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of borax, Sens. Actuators B, 299 (2019) 126936.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications
TrAC Trends Anal. Chem.
